client_context.facility_line: Zurich Cheese Works & Bern Yogurt Plant, Swiss Confederation
client_context.jurisdiction_label: EU & Swiss Sustainability Frameworks for Dairy Manufacturers

executive_summary:
November 2025 marks a significant regulatory shift for Alpine Dairy AG's Swiss and EU operations. The entry into force of the Swiss VOC Ordinance update (CH-VOCV-2025) in March 2025 is now prompting enforcement attention from cantonal authorities, with first inspections of dairy manufacturing facilities scheduled for Q1 2026. Legal counsel recommends an immediate audit of refrigerant and cleaning-agent emission records at both Zurich Cheese Works and Bern Yogurt Plant.

At the EU level, the Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (EU-CSRD-2022/2464) reporting cycle for FY2024 is approaching, with large undertakings required to publish their first ESRS-compliant sustainability statements alongside annual financial reports. As a Swiss dairy exporter operating in the EU single market, Alpine Dairy AG must assess whether its EU-domiciled subsidiaries or branches meet the CSRD in-scope threshold. Internal sustainability teams should confirm entity classification before the 30 June 2026 filing deadline.

The EU Packaging and Packaging Waste Regulation (EU-PPWR-2025/40), which entered into force in February 2025, is advancing through delegated-act drafting. The Commission's first call for evidence on recyclability criteria for multilayer dairy packaging was published this month and directly affects the aseptic and modified-atmosphere packaging used at both Swiss plants. Compliance teams should register for the public consultation scheduled for January 2026.

sections.heading: Swiss VOC Ordinance Update (Verordnung über die Emissionen flüchtiger organischer Verbindungen)
paragraphs: Switzerland's updated VOC Ordinance, effective March 2025, tightens emission limits for volatile organic compounds used in industrial food processing and dairy manufacturing. The revision specifically targets solvent-based cleaning agents and refrigerant top-up procedures that are common in large-scale cheese production. Alpine Dairy's Zurich Cheese Works must review its maintenance records and cross-reference against the new Article 4b thresholds before the first cantonal compliance round in Q1 2026.

The Swiss Federal Council adopted the revised Ordinance on the Reduction of CO2 Emissions (SR 814.318.142.1) with a specific amendment chapter covering food-sector VOC emitters. Dairy facilities with annual throughput exceeding 5,000 tonnes of processed milk equivalents are classified as 'significant VOC emitters' under the revised schedule and must maintain site-level emission inventories updated quarterly. Non-compliance triggers a cantonal administrative levy of CHF 2,000–50,000 depending on excess emission volume. The amendment complements Switzerland's bilateral commitment to align environmental standards with the EU NEC Directive 2016/2284 under the Swiss-EU bilateral agreements framework currently under renegotiation.
sections.callout: Conduct internal VOC emission audit at Zurich Cheese Works and Bern Yogurt Plant against updated thresholds by 31 January 2026.

sections.heading: EU Packaging and Packaging Waste Regulation
paragraphs: The EU PPWR entered into force in February 2025 and sets legally binding recyclability targets for all packaging placed on the EU market, including the multilayer aseptic cartons and modified-atmosphere trays used extensively in dairy export to Germany and Austria. The Commission's first call for evidence on recyclability assessment methodologies was published November 2025 and directly references 'dairy sector multilayer packaging' as a priority category. Compliance timelines escalate through 2030, with mandatory recyclability by design requirements applying from 2030 and extended producer responsibility fee differentials from 2028.

Regulation (EU) 2025/40 (EUR-Lex 32025R0040) establishes a comprehensive packaging framework replacing Directive 94/62/EC. Key provisions affecting dairy exporters include: mandatory minimum recycled content thresholds (10% for contact-sensitive food packaging from 2030, 25% from 2035), recyclability by design requirements with a 10-category scoring system to be defined by delegated act, and reuse targets for distribution packaging. Extended producer responsibility schemes in member states must align with the Regulation's fee modulation methodology by 2028. Swiss-based manufacturers exporting to the EU must comply with PPWR requirements for all packaging units placed on the EU market; Switzerland's own PackV regime is not equivalent and does not exempt exporters from PPWR obligations.
sections.callout: Register for the European Commission's January 2026 public consultation on recyclability criteria; commission packaging lifecycle assessment for export lines to DE and AT by Q2 2026.

sections.heading: Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive
paragraphs: CSRD (Directive 2022/2464/EU) requires large EU undertakings to publish ESRS-compliant sustainability disclosures starting with FY2024 financial years. Alpine Dairy AG's EU sales subsidiaries in Germany and Austria must assess whether their combined balance sheet total, net turnover, and employee count cross the CSRD large-undertaking threshold. If in-scope, the first sustainability statement must be published alongside the 2024 annual report by 30 June 2026, covering mandatory ESRS E1 (climate) and ESRS S1 (own workforce) standards.

CSRD (EUR-Lex 32022L2464) entered into force on 5 January 2023 with staged implementation. Large undertakings (>500 employees or meeting two of three size criteria: balance sheet >EUR 20m, turnover >EUR 40m, employees >250) must report for FY2024, with reports due in 2025 or 2026 depending on fiscal year end. The European Sustainability Reporting Standards (ESRS) adopted under Commission Delegated Regulation (EU) 2023/2772 define 12 topic standards across environmental, social, and governance dimensions. Double materiality assessment is mandatory and must be documented. For dairy sector entities, ESRS E1 (climate change) and ESRS E3 (water and marine resources) are typically material given water intensity of dairy processing and refrigerant GHG emissions. Third-party limited assurance is required for first-year filers, escalating to reasonable assurance by 2028.
sections.callout: Complete CSRD scoping assessment for all EU-domiciled Alpine Dairy entities by 31 December 2025; engage external assurance provider if in-scope.

The Commission's first call for evidence on recyclability assessment methodologies was published November 2025 and directly references 'dairy sector multilayer packaging' as a priority category. Compliance timelines escalate through 2030, with mandatory recyclability by design requirements applying from 2030 and extended producer responsibility fee differentials from 2028.
Register for the European Commission's January 2026 public consultation on recyclability criteria; commission packaging lifecycle assessment for export lines to DE and AT by Q2 2026.

CSRD (Directive 2022/2464/EU) requires large EU undertakings to publish ESRS-compliant sustainability disclosures starting with FY2024 financial years. Alpine Dairy AG's EU sales subsidiaries in Germany and Austria must assess whether their combined balance sheet total, net turnover, and employee count cross the CSRD large-undertaking threshold. If in-scope, the first sustainability statement must be published alongside the 2024 annual report by 30 June 2026, covering mandatory ESRS E1 (climate) and ESRS S1 (own workforce) standards.
Complete CSRD scoping assessment for all EU-domiciled Alpine Dairy entities by 31 December 2025; engage external assurance provider if in-scope.

references.citation: Regulation (EU) 2025/40 on packaging and packaging waste
